Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent throughout the course. I tried to set aside time on weekends, maybe two or three hours each day, but life kept getting in the way. I'd often end up cramming reading materials late at night when anxiety about falling behind hit me hard. I prefer reading and writing things down, so I'd print out notes and work through them at my own pace rather than following any strict schedule.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and how anxious I was feeling about the course. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly I'd have a burst of activity when I realized a deadline was approaching. With four online courses running simultaneously, it was hard to give consistent attention to any single one. I wish I could say I had a regular schedule, but the truth is I was always reacting rather than planning.
Q3: Usually it was fear that prompted me to log in, if I'm being honest. I'd get this anxious feeling that I was missing something important or falling behind my peers. Sometimes I'd log in just to check announcements or see if any deadlines had changed. The gamified tasks they introduced actually helped motivate me a bit more because completing them gave me a small sense of accomplishment.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a natural routine for me. There were brief periods where I'd check in more regularly, especially when the gamified elements were introduced and I wanted to see my progress. But that habit never really stuck because my anxiety would sometimes make me avoid the platform altogether. It felt like a cycle of engaging, then withdrawing when things got overwhelming.
Q5: The reading materials and written content were most useful to me because that's how I learn best. I appreciated when lecturers posted detailed notes or transcripts alongside videos because I could engage with those more deeply. The gamified tasks actually helped break down bigger assignments into smaller, less intimidating pieces. Those felt achievable even when my motivation was low.
Q6: I mostly ignored the video content beyond the first few minutes of each one. Watching videos feels passive to me, and my mind wanders easily when I'm already anxious. Discussion forums were another thing I rarely touched because posting publicly made me uncomfortable. I'd read what others wrote sometimes, but contributing felt too exposed, especially when I wasn't confident in my understanding.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exams. If I knew something would help me perform better on an assessment, I'd prioritize it. The gamified tasks worked well because they gave immediate feedback and made progress visible. Anything that felt vague or disconnected from grades usually got pushed aside.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different, almost like a luxury I couldn't afford. With my anxiety levels and limited motivation, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. Required activities got done because they had to, but optional ones always fell to the bottom of the list. I sometimes felt guilty about skipping them, but survival mode meant focusing only on what counted.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and reading materials, then work through them on paper with a pen. I have notebooks full of handwritten notes that the LMS would never know about. Textbooks and external resources also played a big role, especially when the course materials weren't clicking for me.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing printed notes or rewriting concepts in my own words without ever logging into the platform. My best studying often happened in the library with physical materials spread out in front of me. The LMS would show I was inactive, but I was actually deeply engaged with the content. My exam score reflects that learning even if my online activity doesn't.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach actually. I'd download everything I needed in one session, then spend days working through it offline. Going back to the LMS repeatedly felt inefficient when I could just have everything saved locally. This meant my login frequency probably looked lower than my actual engagement with the materials.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and I just couldn't face the course. When assignments started piling up across all four courses, I felt paralyzed and would avoid logging in entirely. Seeing incomplete tasks and low completion percentages made me feel worse, so sometimes avoidance felt like self-protection. Those were dark weeks where I questioned if I could even finish.
Q13: The workload from managing four online courses simultaneously was brutal on my engagement. When I was fatigued or confused about concepts, the LMS became something I dreaded rather than a helpful tool. Competing responsibilities, including extracurricular commitments, meant the LMS often got whatever energy I had left. Confusion especially hurt because I'd spend time logged in but not actually making progress.
Q14: Many times I'd open materials and stare at them without really absorbing anything. My anxiety would have me scrolling through pages while my mind was elsewhere, worrying about everything I hadn't done. Those sessions probably registered as activity, but they weren't productive at all. It's frustrating because the time was spent, but the learning wasn't happening.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible, like there was this wall between me and the work. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more prepared, but that day rarely came until the deadline was breathing down my neck. It's a cycle I hate but struggle to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine that drove all my LMS activity. Without them, I probably would have engaged even less than I did. I'd typically have a burst of logins and activity in the days before something was due, then go quiet again. The gamified tasks helped because they created smaller, more frequent deadlines that kept me moving.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback boosted my mood and made me want to engage more, which is probably why my post-intervention mood was relatively high. But negative or critical feedback amplified my anxiety and sometimes made me avoid the course for a while. I needed feedback to improve, but receiving it was emotionally difficult.
Q18: The course structure felt a bit overwhelming with so much content spread across different modules. When things were clearly organized and I knew exactly what to do, engagement was easier. But when the path forward was unclear or there were too many options, my anxiety would kick in and I'd freeze. The gamified elements added some helpful structure that guided my participation.
Q19: The gamified tasks genuinely made engagement easier because they broke things into manageable chunks. However, the sheer volume of content and the expectation to watch lengthy videos made things harder for someone like me who prefers reading. Low attendance requirements might seem flexible, but they actually made it easier for me to disengage when anxiety hit. Clearer, text-based pathways would have helped me more.
Q20: I think those students might be like me on my anxious days, logging in and clicking around without actually producing work. High activity could mean someone is lost, confused, or procrastinating by appearing busy. It could also mean they're engaging with content but struggling to translate that into completed assignments. The gap between consuming content and creating work is real.
Q21: This definitely reflects my experience to some degree. My exam score was decent despite my low attendance and task completion rates because I studied heavily offline. The LMS doesn't capture what happens with printed notes at a library desk. Some of us just learn better through methods that don't leave digital footprints, and our performance shows we were actually engaged.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns, time spent on specific pages, and completion of online tasks. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement during logged time, and emotional states affecting learning. They can't tell if someone spent an hour reading deeply or an hour staring blankly while anxious. The numbers tell a partial story at best.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material and trying to understand it, not just completing tasks for the sake of completion. It's about feeling connected to what you're learning and seeing how it matters. For me, engagement happens most when I'm reading and writing in my own space, working through ideas until they click. It's internal and personal, not always visible from the outside.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels or how much courage it sometimes took just to log in. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed materials or the genuine learning that happened away from screens. My emotional journey through the course, the fear, the small victories, the moments of clarity, none of that appears in the data. I'm more than my click patterns suggest.
